coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of lanthanum-mediated carbon quantum dots for the fluorescent detection of aniline and enhancement in photocatalytic degradation efficiency of zirconium oxide nanoparticles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: John, Bony K
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: A unique and efficient fluorescent sensor was developed by doping carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with La(III) ions by microwave treatment. The La-CQDs exhibit an average particle size of 4.8 nm, a quantum yield of 16 % and blue fluorescence emission when excited at 360 nm. Furthermore, the La-CQDs were successfully incorporated into zirconium oxide nanoparticles (La-CQD-ZrO2) and designed as a superior photocatalyst for the degradation of refractory pollutants. Both the sensor and photocatalyst were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), Raman analysis, ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis, dynamic light scattering (DLS), cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and amperometry analysis. As a PL-based sensor, the La-CQDs displayed outstanding selectivity towards toxic chemical aniline with a limit of detection (LOD) of 10 nM within the linear range 0–2 μM. The excellent results obtained in real sample analysis promise the use of La-CQDs as an aniline sensor in industrial-level applications. The photocatalytic performance of La-CQD-ZrO2 was superior to that of pure ZrO2 and CQD-ZrO2 with more than 90 % degradation efficiency towards rhodamine B (Rh B), sunset yellow (SY), methyl orange (MO), 2,4-dichloro phenoxy acetic acid (2,4-D), and cefalexin. The synthesized composite showed excellent promise as a quick and affordable solution for removing industrial dye from wastewater.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of lanthanum-mediated carbon quantum dots for the fluorescent detection of aniline and enhancement in photocatalytic degradation efficiency of zirconium oxide nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686 560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686 560, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A unique and efficient fluorescent sensor was developed by doping carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with La(III) ions by microwave treatment. The La-CQDs exhibit an average particle size of 4.8 nm, a quantum yield of 16 % and blue fluorescence emission when excited at 360 nm. Furthermore, the La-CQDs were successfully incorporated into zirconium oxide nanoparticles (La-CQD-ZrO2) and designed as a superior photocatalyst for the degradation of refractory pollutants. Both the sensor and photocatalyst were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), Raman analysis, ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis, dynamic light scattering (DLS), cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and amperometry analysis. As a PL-based sensor, the La-CQDs displayed outstanding selectivity towards toxic chemical aniline with a limit of detection (LOD) of 10 nM within the linear range 0–2 μM. The excellent results obtained in real sample analysis promise the use of La-CQDs as an aniline sensor in industrial-level applications. The photocatalytic performance of La-CQD-ZrO2 was superior to that of pure ZrO2 and CQD-ZrO2 with more than 90 % degradation efficiency towards rhodamine B (Rh B), sunset yellow (SY), methyl orange (MO), 2,4-dichloro phenoxy acetic acid (2,4-D), and cefalexin. The synthesized composite showed excellent promise as a quick and affordable solution for removing industrial dye from wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanostructured materials have received great attention from researchers because of their particle size-dependent unique thermal, structural, and electronic properties, high scientific attraction and industrial applications. Semiconductor-based nanomaterials (NPs) have found applications in various fields such as sensors, catalysis, photoelectron devices, water splitting, and highly functional and effective devices [1–4]. Quantum dots (QDs) are semiconductor nanomaterials that belong to the zero-dimensional category with a particle size in the 2–10 nm range [5]. Among the various metal and non-metal QDs, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) shows attractive behaviour and characteristics such as low toxicity, inert nature, chemical stability, excellent solubility and tunable photoluminescence by size [6,7]. It can be synthesized by several simple methods such as oxidation of a molecular precursor, electrochemical oxidation, chemical vapour deposition, laser ablation, microwave treatment, electrochemical soaking, and hydrothermal method [8–11]. They are widely used in different fields such as optical sensors, electronic sensors, light emitting devices, photocatalysis, antimicrobial behaviour, bioimaging and water splitting [12].

Aniline is a vital industrial precursor material widely used in the pharmaceutical, commercial, and chemical industries. Aniline is a highly toxic chemical that can cause cancer, mutation, upper respiratory tract irritation, and defects to the central nervous and digestive systems, [13–15]. Aniline is widely produced and used in many industrial sources due to its high demand and excellent raw material for many chemical reactions. Improper treatment of sewage water containing aniline and direct disposal of aniline into the surroundings causes their accumulation and toxic effects in various areas, including surface water, soil, atmosphere, plants, and animals. Hence, it is critical to design a potential sensor that can detect traces of aniline. Aniline has been detected using a variety of methods, including solid-phase micro-extraction [16], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [17], electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [18], liquid chromatography, and gas chromatography. But these methods are typically time-consuming, need expensive instruments, and involve tedious experimental procedures. Recently, photoluminescent sensors have been considered the simplest and least expensive technique for amine detection [19,20]. Even though few reported works are available on fluorescence quenching-based aniline detection, the precursors used in those works are expensive and toxic [21], the synthesis processes used are complicated and time-consuming [22] and detection limits of microscale are possible [23].

Organic dyes, pesticides, and pharmaceutical products are frequently utilized in various industries, such as textile, paper, rubber, fertilizers, plastics, and cosmetics [24,25]. Excess use of them causes environmental pollution and hence several methods have been used to get rid of such organic contaminants, including precipitation, adsorption, coagulation, reverse osmosis, air stripping, and membrane nano-filtration [26]. However, these methods could only change organic contaminants from one phase to another, necessitating additional processing to prevent secondary pollution. Recent studies have demonstrated that semiconductor-based photocatalytic degradation is a superior technique and an efficient alternative to these conventional methods [27]. Semiconductor photocatalysts, such as TiO2, ZnO, SrTiO3, ZnS, and ZrO2 are excellent options for this purpose. ZrO2 is a semiconductor nanomaterial with significant negative conduction band potential, extensive bandgap energy between 3.25–5.1 eV, and good stability [28]. The practical use of ZrO2 as a visible light-assisted photocatalyst is restricted by the high bandgap energy, which allows photoexcited electrons to quickly recombine with holes and slows down the overall efficiency of solar energy conversion. Modification techniques such as adding noble metals into the semiconductor matrix, doping, and coupling with semiconductors that have a narrower bandgap have all been used to solve this problem [29,30]. Doping the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with heteroatoms is the most efficient, convenient, and fast strategy to enhance the QY and fluorescent properties [31]. It allows enhanced specific interaction of CQDs with the analyte molecules and improves the composite formation power [32]. The doping with rare earth elements, especially with La(III) ions, is desirable since they exhibit large spin–orbit coupling constant, high atomic radii, availability of free electrons, low toxicity, and availability of several unoccupied orbitals compared to other metallic atoms [33]. Incorporating CQDs into semiconductor nanoparticles has proven to be an inventive way to increase their photocatalytic efficiency [34]. Since CQDs possess a conjugated structure, a strong interaction exists between the semiconductor nanoparticles and the CQD, stabilising the composite and enabling the charge transfer process, which results in improved photocatalytic performance [29]. Modifying ZrO2 by doped CQDs compared to an undoped one will further enhance the photocatalytic activity of the composite due to the narrow band gap and enhance electron-hole separation power. The availability of La(III) ions allows the formation of a type-II heterojunction between the CQDs and ZrO2, further preventing electron-hole recombination [35].

The objectives of this work include the synthesis of La-CQDs as an effective fluorescent sensor for toxic aniline molecules by the microwave-assisted method and the design of La-CQD modified ZrO2 nanocomposite (La-CQD-ZrO2) as a useful visible light assisted photocatalyst for the removal of dyes, pesticides and pharmaceutical products from the water sources. Only some works have been reported on the PL-based detection of aniline in the literature. Here, the developed La-CQD sensor with a limit of detection (LOD) of 10 nM, within the 0–2 μM linear range. The performance of the La-CQD sensor in the real water sample also yields satisfactory results with an excellent recovery percentage. This is a first time report on the successful addition of CQDs enriched with La(III) ions into the ZrO2 nanostructure through the microwave-assisted method. The prepared La-CQD-ZrO2 composites show highly extended visible light absorption and stabilized visible light photocatalytic activity for pollutant degradation. The role of La-CQDs in the degradation pathways, efficiency of the reaction, characterization of the developed composite and a probable visible light-assisted photocatalytic mechanism are presented in detail.

Glucose (C6H12O6), lanthanum nitrate (La(NO3)3), sodium citrate (Na3C6H₅O₇), zirconyl chloride (ZrOCl2·8H2O), sodium acetate (CH3COONa), acetone, dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), nitrobenzene, benzene, pyridine, chloroform, tetrahydrofuran (THF), toluene, rhodamine B (Rh B), methyl orange (XO), sunset yellow (SY), 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D), and cefalexin. All the reagents were purchased from Merck, India.

The CQDs were synthesized by mixing 10 mL glucose (1 M) with 5 mL of sodium citrate (0.5 M). The resultant suspension was heated at 600 W for 10 min in a cyclic mode using a microwave oven to obtain a brownish-yellow CQD solution. La-CQDs were fabricated by mixing 10 mL of 0.3 M lanthanum nitrate with the formerly prepared CQDs suspension at room temperature. The reaction mixture was kept in the microwave oven at 600 W for 6 min in a cyclic mode. Finally, the obtained brownish-yellow La-CQD solution was filtered and freeze-dried to obtain powdered La-CQDs.

ZrO2 nanostructures were synthesized using the hydrothermal method. Typically, 50 mL ZrOCl2·8H2O (0.3 M) and 20 mL of CH3COONa (0.2 M) solutions were mixed and the mixture was magnetically stirred for 1 h. The solution was taken in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was left to cool down to normal temperature. The products were centrifuged, collected and washed many times with a mixture of deionized water and ethanol. Finally, the white ZrO2 products were obtained by drying them at 80 °C for 2 h.

The developed ZrO2 (0.5 g) and La-CQD (0.025 g) were dissolved separately in 20 mL of deionized water and stirred for 30 min to obtain a clear solution. This was placed in a domestic microwave oven for 10 min at 600 W. A solid powder was obtained after complete evaporation of the liquid. The obtained La-CQD-ZrO2 was washed well with ethanol and dried in an oven at 80 °C for 2 h. All the steps involved in the synthesis process is schematic illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the developed La-CQDs was examined using quinine sulphate with a QY of 54% as the standard, according to the equation,
(1)

where ΦS is the QY of the La-CQDs and ΦR is the QY of the reference. The slopes of the integrated fluorescence intensity versus the absorbance plot of the sample and reference are denoted by μS and μR, respectively. The refractive indices of the sample and reference solution are represented as ηs and ηR, respectively.

The fluorescent detection of aniline was performed in 0.3 mg/mL La-CQD solution at pH 7 in a phosphate buffer solution (PBS) with a contact period of 2 min. The final volume of the aniline-La-CQD-PBS mixture was fixed at 3 mL. The fluorescence quenching owes to the effective interaction between La-CQDs and aniline. The sensitivity and LOD studies were performed in the 0–2 μM concentrations of aniline and the change in PL intensity was plotted. The selectivity of the method was explored by mixing about 30 μM interfering molecules into the La-CQD solution containing 10 μM aniline molecules and the corresponding PL intensity was measured after an contact period of 2 min.

The photocatalytic behaviour of the La-CQD-ZrO2 composite was studied by mixing approximately 0.025 g of the catalyst with 30 mL of dye solution (10 mg/L) or 2,4-D or cefalexin (20 mg/L), into a 100 mL beaker. The mixture was subjected to 30 min of sonication, followed by 30 min of magnetic stirring in the dark, to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. About 3 mL of the solution was taken and labelled as dark before being exposed to sunlight. After the catalyst-dye mixture was exposed to sunlight, 3 mL of the solution was taken out at regular intervals and the absorbance was recorded using a UV–vis. spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: La-CQD as a fluorescence sensor for aniline

ce:section.ce:para:
UV–vis. absorption and photo luminous studies were performed to explore the optical behaviour of the developed La-CQDs [36]. The UV–vis. spectra of fabricated La-CQDs (Fig. 1
a) showed an intense band at 275 nm resulting from the π–π* transition in sp2 hybridized bonds. La-CQDs exhibits maximum PL emission at 438 nm when exciting the sample at 360 nm (Fig. 1 a). The La-CQD solution appeared brown under visible and emits blue fluorescence under UV light at 360 nm as depicted in the inset photograph in Fig. 1 a. To optimize the excitation wavelength, the PL emission spectra of the La-CQDs were recorded by increasing the wavelength from 330 nm to 410 nm. The emission spectra of La-CQDs in Fig. 1 b displayed a red shift in the emission maximum, with a maximum PL emission at 438 nm when exciting the sample at 360 nm. Hence, excitation wavelength can significantly affect the fluorescence behaviour of the La-CQDs [37]. The calculated QY of the system was about 16 %. The doping with La(III) and the presence of electron-rich auxochromic functional groups on the CQD surface was responsible for the good QY value.

The morphology and size distribution of the fabricated La-CQDs were investigated using TEM analysis. La-CQDs are well separated, monodispersed and have a spherical shape as depicted in Fig. 2
a. HR-TEM image in Fig. 2 b shows some sort of crystallinity inside the individual quantum dots with a fringe width of 0.19 nm, which is comparable to the lattice spacing in graphitic carbon atoms [38]. However, the system as a whole is amorphous as apparent from the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern shown in Fig. 2 c. The average particle size of the La-CQDs was determined from the particle size distribution histogram (Fig. 2 d). The size of the quantum dots distributed between 2 and 8 nm range, with an average size of 4.8 nm. The average particle size obtained in the dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique, was about 8.95 nm, which is comparable to the particle size determined in the TEM analysis ( Fig. S1 ).

The FT-IR spectrum of CQDs ( Fig. 3
a), displays strong broad band at 3,377 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of the –OH group. The typical vibrational peaks of the carbonyl group and CC bond was observed at 1714 and 1648 cm−1, respectively. The band originating from the C-O stretching and bending vibrations was observed at 1157 and 1030 cm−1, respectively. However, in the La-CQDs, the –OH vibrational band was shifted to 3260 cm−1. In addition, CC and CO band was merged to a single peak at 1630 cm−1. A shift in the C-O stretching and bending peaks was also noticed at 1232 and 1074 cm−1, respectively. This shift in the FT-IR bands was due to the successful coordination of La(III) ions with the surface oxygen-containing functional groups in CQDs [39,40].

The amorphous structure of the developed La-CQDs was further confirmed from the XRD spectrum given in Fig. 3 b. The XRD pattern showed a broad peak at 2θ = 24.64°, which corresponds to the specified (002) graphitic plane (JCPDS No. 26–1076) [38,39]. The Raman analysis of the La-CQDs exhibited a D band at 1355 cm−1 and a G band at 1580 cm−1 (Fig. 3 c) [41]. The obtained ID/IG ratio of 0.85 confirm the availability of highly graphitized carbon atoms in the developed quantum dots [42].

The XPS survey spectrum shows that La-CQDs are primarily consist of carbon, oxygen and lanthanum with the characteristic binding peaks at 285.3, 530.58 and 837.5 eV ( Fig. 4
a). The elemental composition of carbon, oxygen, and lanthanum in the sample was about 52.4, 40.8, and 6.8 %, respectively. The C 1 s spectrum exhibits three peaks at 282.9, 284.88, and 287.52 eV, attributed to the C-C, CC, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 4 b). Three peaks at 529.56, 532.03, and 533.01 eV in the O 1 s spectrum ( Fig. 4 c) are ascribed to C-O, C-O-C and CO, respectively [43]. The deconvoluted spectrum of La 3d in Fig. 4 d unveiled two binding energy peaks at 837.17 and 854.30 eV associated with La 3d5/2 and La 3d3/2, respectively. The results of both XPS and FT-IR significantly corroborate and confirm the presence of La in the developed La-CQDs.

The stability analysis of quantum dots is very essential for effective practical applications and improved efficiency of detection. The photoluminescence emission intensities of La-CQDs with changed ionic strengths of KCl ranging from 0 to 3 M did not exhibit any notable change in the emission intensity as shown in Fig. S2 a. The stability of La-CQDs was analyzed at various pH ranges from 3 to 12 ( Fig. S2 b) confirming that photoluminescence properties are not very much affected in different pH of the medium. The La-CQDs irradiated with UV light for different time intervals also do not affect the fluorescent behaviour of the material (Fig. S2 c). The shelf life of La-CQDs was analysed by storing the quantum dots at 4 °C for twelve weeks and the PL intensities were measured at various time intervals (Fig. S2 d). The observed stability upon storage indicates the possible multifaceted applications of CQDs. Hence, the developed La-CQDs exhibit exceptional stability to different ionic strengths, pH-resistant emission behaviour, excellent photostability, and long storage time without agglomeration.

To achieve maximum detection power of the sensor, the reaction parameters such as pH of the medium and interaction time between the sensor and analyte were optimized. The effect of pH on the PL spectra of La-CQD solution containing 2 μM aniline was examined, and a plot of relative fluorescence response (F0/F) of La-CQDs towards aniline at different pH values is given in Fig. S3 a. The sensor exhibited outstanding quenching in fluorescence in various pH mediums and the highest quenching effect was observed at a pH of 7. The reduced PL quenching observed at acidic and basic medium was due to the protonation of the surface groups and partial hydrolysis of aniline molecules, respectively [44]. The PL spectra of the La-CQD-aniline mixture at different time interval is demonstrated in Fig. S3 b. The interaction between La-CQDs and aniline was rapid and completed within 2 min. Hence there is no need of strict time control during the sensing procedure.

The sensitivity of the developed fluorescent sensor was examined by mixing 2 μM of different molecules such as acetone, dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, nitrobenzene, aniline, benzene, pyridine, chloroform, tetrahydrofuran (THF), and toluene into 0.30 mg/mL La-CQD solution at pH 7. A significant quenching in PL intensity was detected only in the case of the La-CQD-aniline mixture as illustrated in the relative fluorescence response (F/F0) versus various analytes plots in Fig. 5
a. None of the other analytes shows any significant quenching in the PL intensity of the sensor.

The linear range and LOD of the method can be evaluated using the Stern-Volmer equation,
(2) F0/F = 1 + Ksv [Q]

Where F0 and F represent the PL intensities of La-CQDs in the absence and presence of aniline molecules at 438 nm. [Q] denotes the concentration of aniline and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. As the concentration of aniline increases from 0 to 5 μM, a gradual quenching in the PL intensities of La-CQDs was observed, as shown in Fig. 5 b. From this analysis, a F0/F against aniline concentration plot was drawn (Fig. 5 c) and the linear range of detection was estimated as 0 to 2 μM. The LOD of the method towards aniline detection was calculated using the equation,
(3) LOD = 3σ/m

The obtained LOD was about 10 nM. The surface of the CQD is negatively charged (-34.6 eV) as evident from the zeta potential analysis (Fig. 5 d) due to the availability of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of quantum dots. After the successful addition of La(III) into the CQD surface, the value reduced to −11.7 mV (Fig. 5 e). The decrease in the negative surface charge was due to the effective binding of positively charged La(III) with the negatively charged functional groups on the CQD surface. The binding of aniline into the La-CQD surface causes a further decrease in the zeta potential value (−1.2 mV) due to an attractive electrostatic interaction between positively charged anilinium cations and various functional groups on the La-CQD surface. Table 1 compares the sensing capability of the La-CQD sensor with those of previously reported works and shows that the detection ability and selectivity of the proposed sensor were found to be superior to that of many recently published works.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity analysis and quenching mechanism of La-CQD towards aniline
ce:section.ce:para:
The selectivity of the sensor towards aniline was investigated by mixing 3 μM aniline with 10 μM of other interfering molecules such as acetone, dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), nitrobenzene, benzene, pyridine, chloroform, tetrahydrofuran (THF), and toluene in pH 7 with an interaction time of 2 min. The sensor exhibited excellent selectivity towards aniline, and the impact on the fluorescence quenching is negligible by the interfering analytes (Fig. 6
a).

Quenching in fluorescence can happen as a result of two phenomena, static or dynamic quenching. Static quenching involves the formation of a ground-state complex between the fluorophore and the quenchers. The dynamic quenching owes to the excited-state interaction between both fluorophores and quenchers. Fluorescence lifetime analysis of the pure La-CQDs and La-CQD-aniline mixture was performed to identify the quenching mechanism (Fig. 6 b). The excited state lifetime of La-CQD was about 4.78 ns and La-CQD-aniline mixture was about 4.71 ns. Hence, there was no considerable change in the PL lifetime observed before and after mixing with aniline, and the possibility of static quenching was confirmed. Furthermore, the UV–vis. spectrum of La-CQDs, aniline, La-CQD-aniline mixture and PL excitation spectra of La-CQDs is shown in Fig. S4 . Aniline displayed two broad absorption bands at 280 and 230 nm and in the absorption spectrum of La-CQD-aniline mixture, a shift in the position of the absorption peak of aniline was observed. This indicates the formation of a stable complex between La-CQDs and aniline and confirms the occurrence of a static quenching mechanism [48].

Aniline was found in small concentrations in some foods, fabrics, and water sources. The developed La-CQDs sensor was used to analyze the amount of aniline present in the real samples of tap and river water. The analysis results obtained for the water samples spiked with known amounts of aniline is given in Table 2 . The developed methods achieved good recoveries ranging from 99.1 to 102.1%. These results suggest that the developed procedure is efficient and precise for recognizing aniline in real water sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic applications of La-CQD modified ZrO2

ce:section.ce:para:
The FT-IR spectrum of ZrO2 nanoparticles ( Fig. 7
a) exhibited a broad band at 3418 cm−1 correlated with the –OH stretching vibrations. Sharp bands at 1542 and 1381 cm−1 are assigned to the –OH bending vibrations [49]. The band situated at 660 cm−1 is due to the Zr-O vibration of the tetragonal structure and the width of the band indicates that the ZrO2 powders are nanocrystals [50]. FT-IR analysis of La-CQDs confirms the presence of -CO and -O-H functional groups on the La-CQD surface which allows excellent water-dispersible properties. The broad vibrational band at 3300 cm−1 corresponds to the -O-H stretching vibration. The bands at 1580, 1385, 1250, and 1060 cm−1, represent symmetric stretching bands of CO/CC, asymmetric vibrations of C-H, and vibrational bands of C-O bonds, respectively. In addition, the highly intense bands in the range of 1700–1000 cm−1 simply indicate the successful functionalization of La(III) ions into the carbon framework [51]. The FT-IR bands obtained for 6,7 and 8 % La-CQD-ZrO2 demonstrated a shift in the functional group vibrations, which confirms the effective addition of La-CQDs into the ZrO2 particles. The broad -O-H band was shifted to 3106 from 3300 cm−1 in the developed composite. In addition, the FT-IR bands of CO, CC, C-H, C-O, and Zr-O bonds were also shifted, and the new peaks were observed at 1688, 1605, 1304, 1208, and 600 cm−1, respectively. The FT-IR peak positions of all composition of La-CQD-ZrO2 appears the same; however, more intense peaks were observed in the case of 8 % La-CQD-ZrO2 composite.

The XRD patterns of La-CQDs, pure ZrO2 and La-CQD modified ZrO2 samples are shown in Fig. 7 b. La-CQDs revealed a broad pattern at 2θ = 24.150, corresponding to the (002) graphitic plane which indicates the amorphous behaviour of the quantum dots [52]. The XRD patterns of pure ZrO2 powder and ZrO2 powder incorporated with La-CQDs exhibited patterns at 2θ values 23.6, 31.5, 33.5, and 48.50 originating from the (110), (101), (120), and (112) crystalline lattice planes, respectively. The peaks corresponding to the La-CQDs are not observable due to the very low concentration of La-CQDs in the developed composite. Comparing the obtained XRD pattern with the previously reported data shows that the developed ZrO2 and La-CQD-ZrO2 system exhibits a tetragonal crystalline structure with JCPDS No. 81–1544 [53].

The FESEM analysis was performed to examine the surface morphology of the developed photocatalyst. The FESEM image ( Fig. 8
a) of the developed ZrO2 NPs shows grains of ZrO2 which are randomly arranged with a homogeneous spherical shape. In the La-CQD-ZrO2 binary composite, the lattice spaces of the spherical ZrO2 NPs are filled with the La-CQD molecules as observed in Fig. 8 b.

The formation of La-CQD-ZrO2 was further confirmed by the TEM analysis. The generation of highly aggregated spherical ZrO2 nanomaterials is depicted in Fig. 9
a. The heterogeneous nucleation in the system led to the formation of the agglomerated ZrO2 NPs. The successful incorporation of La-CQDs into the ZrO2 crystal structure was confirmed by the TEM image of La-CQD-ZrO2 shown in Fig. 9 b, in which spherical La-CQDs are randomly spread over the surface of ZrO2 particles. Lattice fringes were visible for both ZrO2 (0.30 nm) and La-CQDs (0.19 nm) in the HR-TEM images of La-CQD-ZrO2 in Fig. 9 c, suggesting the tetragonal crystalline structure of the composite. Furthermore, the SAED pattern (Fig. 9 d) displays bright circular rings due to the crystalline nature of the produced composite. From the particle size distribution diagram of La-CQD-ZrO2 (
Fig. S5
), the average particle size is distributed between 28 and 42 nm range, with an average size of 37 nm.

The chemical composition and elemental identification of the composite were achieved by using XPS analysis. The survey spectrum of La-CQD-ZrO2 (Fig. 10
a) shows four individual peaks resulting from the availability of elements Zr 3d, La 3d, C1s, and O1s. The deconvoluted XPS spectrum of Zr 3d displayed three peaks (Fig. 10 b) at 180.45, 181.76 and 184.20 eV, originating from the Zr-O 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 states. The deconvoluted La 3d spectrum (Fig. 10 c) showed two peaks at 836, and 854 eV derived from the La 3d5/2 and La 3d3/2 moieties, respectively. Three peaks obtained in the C 1 s spectrum at 283.68, 284.82, and 287.08 eV was assigned to the C-C, CC, and CO/OC-O species (Fig. 10 d). Four separate peaks were obtained in the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 10 e) at 529.44, 530.12, 531.94 and 532.92 eV, attributed to the C-O, Zr-O, C-O-C and CO bonds, respectively.

The optical behaviour of the composite was studied through UV–vis. diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) (Fig. 11
a). The pure ZrO2 displayed a distinct absorption peak at 332 nm. The absorbance band of CQD exhibited a broad band at 278 nm due to π-π* transitions, and La-CQD shows a red shift in the absorption band at 287 nm. The modification of ZrO2 by the La-CQDs causes a red shift in the absorption wavelength from 332 nm to 347 nm with an enhanced absorbance. The Tauc plot (Fig. 11 b) and the Kubelka-Munk equation were used to determine the band gap of the ZrO2, La-CQD, and La-CQD-ZrO2. The Kubelka-Munk equation is given as,
(4) ∝hϑ = A(hϑ − Eg)1/n
where α, h, and ϑ are the absorption coefficient, Planck’s constant and light frequency, respectively. A and Eg denote proportionality constant, and band gap energy values. Depending on the kind of optical transition that a semiconductor undergoes, the value of n changes as 1/2 for direct transition and 2 for indirect transition. The obtained band gap was about 3.4 eV for ZrO2 and was reduced to 3.03 eV on modifying with CQD- ZrO2. Further modification of ZrO2 with La-CQDs resulted in a band gap of 2.70, 2.65, and 2.68 eV for 6, 7 and 8 % La-CQD-ZrO2 composite. The reduced band gap of La-CQD-ZrO2 compared to pure ZrO2 allows for a red shift in absorption and enhanced photocatalytic activities due to the availability of more number of active reactive species in the medium and their lower tendency for recombination [54]. The valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) potentials were evaluated using the equations,
(5) EVB = χ - Ee + 0.5Eg
(6) ECB = EVB - Eg
here EVB denotes valence band potential, χ is the electronegativity of the material, Ee represents the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale, and Eg denotes the measured band gap of the developed photocatalyst [55]. From Eqs. (4) and (5), the calculated value of EVB and ECB is about 0.085, and −2.565 eV, respectively.

Any factor which decreases the rate of recombination of the photogenerated charge carrier would increase the efficiency of the photocatalyst. PL analysis was performed to gain a better understanding of the recombination rate of photoexcited charge carriers (Fig. 11 c) [56]. The PL emission spectra of pure ZrO2 exhibited strong fluorescence at 435 nm which indicates fast recombination of the charge carrier. The La-CQDs display PL emission intensity less than that of Pure ZrO2. The addition of La-CQDs significantly reduces the intensity of the ZrO2 emission peak. The efficient electron migration from the excited state ZrO2 to the CQDs through the La(III) ions was attributed to the significant PL quenching. As a result, the interaction between ZrO2, CQDs, and La (III) ions in the La-CQD-ZrO2 nanocomposite could contribute to efficient separation, extending charge carrier lifetime and improving photodegradation efficiency. Among the composites with different La-CQD concentrations, the 7% La-CQD-ZrO2 system demonstrated the greatest PL quenching, implying that 7% La-CQD was the optimal concentration to achieve enhanced photoactivity of the catalyst [57]. The PL spectrum of ZrO2 and La-CQDs was observed at 435 and 433 nm, respectively. The PL spectrum of La-CQD-ZrO2 displayed a blue shift in the peak position to 431 nm. This slight shift in the peak position is attributed to the polydispersive nature of the La-CQD-ZrO2 composite. The average size of the quantum dots was about 4.8 nm and the average size of ZrO2 nanoparticles was around 40 nm. Hence the presence of particles having different sizes and physiochemical behaviour affects their electronic structure, resulting in a blue shift in the PL spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of the La-CQD-ZrO2 composite
ce:section.ce:para:
Rhodamine B (Rh B) was used to evaluate the photocatalytic abilities of pure ZrO2, CQD-ZrO2 and the La-CQD-ZrO2 composite under visible light irradiation. The results of photocatalytic activity analysis given in Fig. 12
a,b & c clearly show that the activity of the ZrO2 was boosted when modified with CQDs, and a further increase in activity was observed when modified with La-CQDs. The degradation efficiency was calculated using the equation,
Degradation (%) = [(C0 – Ct) × 100]/C0
where C0 is the initial concentration of the analyte and Ct is the concentration of the same at time t. The calculated degradation efficiencies for the ZrO2, CQD-ZrO2, and La-CQD-ZrO2 catalysts were found to be 62, 78 and 93%, respectively.

The photodegradation of Rh B was performed using La-CQD-ZrO2 composite with various La-CQD compositions to optimise the composition of the catalyst (
Fig. S6
a,b&c). In comparison to those with 6 and 8 wt%, Rh B undergoes maximum degradationin the sample containing 7 wt% composite, with a degradation efficiency of 93 %. The C/C0 vs time graph (Fig. S 7a) was used to further confirm the improved degradation ability of the catalyst. Where C0 is the initial concentration of the analyte and Ct is the concentration of the same at time t. -ln C/C0 vs time graph (Fig. S 7b) was used to follow the reaction kinetics, and the photocatalytic degradation reaction was found to follow pseudo-first-order kinetics with a rate constant of 0.105 min−1 according to the equation,
(7) -ln C/C0 = kt
where k and t denote the rate constant and time of the reaction [58].

Further studies show that the designed photocatalyst was found to be efficient for the successful degradation of pollutants including organic dyes such as methyl orange (MO), and sunset yellow (SY), organochlorine pesticide 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D), and pharmaceutical product cefalexin.

Similar to Rh B degradation, the absorption spectra of SY and MO (Fig. 13
a&b) showed excellent degradation ability of the developed composite with a degradation efficiency of 92 and 94 %. To find out the degree of mineralization of the degraded dyes, TOC analysis was performed before and after the reaction. The TOC bar diagram in Fig. S8 , shows a TOC removal of 94, 90, and 89 % for Rh B, SY, and MO, respectively. This demonstrates that the developed photocatalyst decolourized and mineralized these dyes rapidly and almost completely.

HPLC, mass, and TOC analysis were performed to study the degradation of 2,4-D and cefalexin. The HPLC examination of pure 2,4-D revealed a sharp peak at a retention time of 5 min ( Fig. 14
a). After 90 min of continuous interaction with the photocatalyst, the 2,4-D molecules were completely degraded into small less toxic products and a new peak of the degradation product was observed at a retention time of 2.4 min. The HPLC profile of cefalexin revealed a sharp peak with a retention time of 7.7 min ( Fig. 14 b). The strength of the peak was decreased rapidly and a new peak was observed after 30 min of visible light irradiation, showing the degradation of cefalexin into other less hazardous degraded products.

The chemical behavior and molecular mass of the degradation products were determined using High-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) analysis. The mass spectrum of pure 2,4-D showed a sharp peak at m/z = 218.95. After the degradation the peak at 218.95 disappeared completely and a new peak was observed at m/z 176.95. The degradation product was 2,4-dichloro anisole, less hazardous than 2,4-D ( Fig. S9
a&b). The drug molecule cefalexin exhibited a strong peak at m/z = 347.77 and during photoirradiation degraded into a less hazardous benzene derivative with m/z of 138.05 ( Fig. S10
a&b). The TOC analysis of the samples ( Fig. S11 ) revealed a TOC removal of 90, and 88 %, by the catalysis on 2,4-D, and cefalexin, respectively. The value of TOC demonstrates that there has been significant pollutant degradation in the reaction medium.

The recyclability and stability of the photocatalyst must be checked for practical applications. To examine the stability of La-CQD-ZrO2 both the FT-IR spectrum and XRD pattern were recorded. The XRD pattern in Fig. 15
a showed only a slight change in the peak intensities after the photocatalytic reaction. The FT-IR spectrum of La-CQD-ZrO2 before and after the photodegradation reaction do not show any major changes (Fig. 15 b). The long reusable life of the catalyst was further confirmed by the C/C0 vs. time graph in Fig. 15 c. The recycling experiments under identical conditions exhibited only a small change in the photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared photocatalyst even after 4 cycling times. These results allow the future industrial applications of the developed catalyst as a solution to different environmental pollution.

To propose a plausible mechanism of degradation, scavenger and electrochemical analysis were performed. The scavenger study was carried out using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) as a scavenger for holes (h+), superoxide radical (.O2 –), and hydroxyl radicals (.OH), respectively. The plot in Fig. 15 d shows that the photodegradation ability of the active species diminishes in the order OH. > O2 .- > h+. Thus, hydroxyl radicals are the most active species involved in the photodegradation process.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), amperometry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques were used to study the electrochemical behaviour of the La-CQD-ZrO2 composite. The CV studies were achieved by using a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode in a mixture of 5 mM[Fe(CN)6]3-/[Fe(CN)6]4 and 0.1 M KCl as the electrolyte. By drop casting La-CQDs, ZrO2, and La-CQD-ZrO2 on the GCE surface, the cyclic voltammograms associated with these materials were recorded separately (Fig. 16
a). Both La-CQDs and ZrO2 exhibited well-defined oxidation and reduction peaks. However, the maximum area of the cyclic voltammogram was observed when coating the GCE surface with La-CQD-ZrO2 nanocomposite. Hence, modifying with La-CQDs leads to an enhanced electrical conductivity in the ZrO2 nanoparticles.

Using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the charge-carrying capability and resistance of the developed composite were examined. The high resistance and low charge-carrying power of the La-CQDs, and ZrO2, were confirmed from their large radius semicircled Nyquist plots. The La-CQD-ZrO2 composite displayed lower resistance and higher conductivity than the unmodified La-CQDs and ZrO2 as apparent from the Nyquist plot with a low semicircular radius (Fig. 16 b). This is because there are more free electrons and holes available in the modified system [59]. To assess the activity of the generated composite when exposed to visible light, photocurrent measurements have been performed using the amperometric technique. The amperometric curve of La-CQDs, ZrO2, and La-CQD-ZrO2 composite in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution irradiated with a Xenon lamp (300 W) is illustrated in Fig. 16 c. Compared to unmodified ZrO2 and La-CQDs, the modified binary composite has the highest photocurrent value. The photocurrent value is increased by the formation of free charge carriers in the system as a result of light irradiation.

The scavenger study shows that hydroxyl (OH.) radicals are the most active species present in the medium for the photodegradation of refractory pollutants. From the UV–vis. DRS analysis, the optical band gap of ZrO2 is about 3.4 eV and shows very strong absorption in the ultraviolet light region but without absorbing the light in the visible region [60]. The modification of ZrO2 by the La-CQDs resulted in a reduced band gap of 2.65 eV, allowing the developed composite to absorb from the visible region of light. It is due to the up-conversion characteristic and excellent light harvesting capacity of the La-CQD-ZrO2 composite.

When placed under sunlight, photogenerated holes and electrons are produced in the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) of ZrO2. Since CQDs exhibit photo-induced electron migration property, the photo-generated electrons are transferred from the CB of ZrO2 to the CQDs followed by the La(III) ions leaving holes in the valence band and inhibiting electron-hole recombination. The high separation efficiency considerably increases the photocatalytic activity of La-CQD-ZrO2 by extending the lifespan of photogenerated charge carriers by avoiding electron and hole recombination.

The calculated band edge potentials EVB and ECB of La-CQD-ZrO2 composite were 0.085 and −2.565 eV, respectively. Since the calculated EVB of La-CQD-ZrO2 is greater than the usual redox potential of.OH/OH– (1.99 eV vs NHE), a large number of active hydroxyl radicals are available in the system with excellent degradation power. Photogenerated electrons on the surface of CQDs and La(III) ions react with adsorbed oxygen to form O2 .- radicals radical anions, which on further interaction with protons from water molecules form highly reactive hydroxyl radicals. The holes in the valence band also produce hydroxyl radicals when they interact with adsorbed water or surface hydroxyl groups. These highly oxidizing ⋅OH radicals photodegrade the organic pollutants into low molecular weight and less toxic organics, CO2 and water effectively and efficiently. A simplified possible mechanism of photocatalytic degradation is graphically given in Scheme 2 .
(8) ZrO2 + hυ → ZrO2(h+) + ZrO2(e-)
(9) ZrO2(e-) + CQDs → ZrO2 + CQDs(e-)
(10) CQDs (e-) + La(III) → CQDs + La(III)(e-)
(11) La(III)(e-) →.O2 – + La(III)
(12)
.O2 – + H2O → OH. + O2 –
(13) ZrO2(h+) → OH. + ZrO2

A facile and affordable procedure was developed for the production of La-CQDs and La-CQD modified ZrO2 composite by microwave treatment. The La-CQD exhibits sensitive and selective PL-based sensing ability towards aniline molecules with a LOD of 10 nM in the 0–2 μM linear range. The real sample analysis of the aniline using the developed sensor also yields satisfactory results. The developed La-CQD-ZrO2 nanocomposite showed superior photocatalytic activity against rhodamine B, sunset yellow, methyl orange, 2,4-D and cefalexin with a degradation efficiency of more than 90%. On the contrary, pure ZrO2 and CQD-ZrO2 showed low degradation ability compare to that of La-CQD-ZrO2. The successful action of La(III) ions and CQD on the ZrO2 surface, prevents the quick recombination of electron-hole pairs, which considerably enhances the photocatalytic action of the ZrO2 composite. Hence, the designed fluorescent sensor and photocatalyst will have promising applications in the selective detection of aniline and efficient removal of refractory pollutants from the wastewater.
Bony K John: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Beena Mathew: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
